paragraphs.text: SRD-46 does show **very strong Pb²⁺ binding by aminopolycarboxylate ligands**, with the main top-affinity names in the retrieved Pb(II) set being **DTPA, CDTA, and EDTA**, followed by related EDTA/HEDTA-like chelators. Among ligands with clear clinical relevance to **lead poisoning**, **EDTA** is the standout: the therapeutic agent is **CaNa₂EDTA**, an EDTA-family chelator.

paragraphs.text:
## Highest-affinity Pb²⁺ ligands found in SRD-46
From the Pb²⁺ (`metal_125`) stability results, the strongest named chelator families identified were:

| Rank tier | Ligand / family in SRD-46 | Clinical use note |
|---|---|---|
| Top tier | DTPA (diethylenetriaminepentaacetate) | Used clinically mainly for some radionuclides, **not standard for lead poisoning** |
| Top tier | CDTA (trans-1,2-cyclohexylenediaminetetraacetate) | No standard clinical use for lead poisoning identified here |
| Top tier | EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetate) | **Yes** — clinically relevant as **CaNa₂EDTA** for lead poisoning |
| Strong, below top tier | O-bis(ethylenenitrilo)tetraacetate and related ether-linked aminopolycarboxylates | No standard clinical role for lead poisoning identified here |
| Strong, below top tier | HEDTA-type ligands | No standard clinical role for lead poisoning identified here |
| Lower than the aminopolycarboxylate top tier | Citrate | Not a primary clinical chelator for lead poisoning |

| Lower than the aminopolycarboxylate top tier | Citrate | Not a primary clinical chelator for lead poisoning |

paragraphs.text:
## Clinically used ligands appearing or highlighted in the Pb²⁺ dataset/context
The retrieved SRD-46 context also flagged sulfur-donor chelators and older clinical agents:

| Ligand | SRD-46 Pb²⁺ relevance from retrieved results | Clinical use note |
|---|---|---|
| EDTA | Among the strongest Pb²⁺ binders | **Yes** — **CaNa₂EDTA** is an established lead chelator |
| D-penicillamine | Present for Pb²⁺, but not highlighted as top-affinity versus EDTA/DTPA/CDTA | Has been used clinically, but not a leading modern first-choice option |
| Tiopronin (MPG) | Present in older Pb²⁺ subset | Clinically used thiol drug, but not a standard lead-poisoning mainstay |
| 2-mercaptoethanol / related thiols | Present as Pb²⁺ binders | Not standard clinical lead-chelation agents |
| DTPA | Strong Pb²⁺ affinity | Clinical use exists, but chiefly for radionuclide decorporation rather than lead |

paragraphs.text:
## Bottom line
| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Which ligands in SRD-46 have the highest affinity for Pb²⁺? | **DTPA, CDTA, and EDTA** are the top Pb²⁺ chelator families identified in the retrieved SRD-46 results. |
| Are any used clinically? | **Yes** — **EDTA**, specifically **CaNa₂EDTA**, is the key clinically established chelator for lead poisoning. |
| Any others with clinical relevance? | **D-penicillamine** has clinical history, but it was **not** a top-affinity leader in the retrieved Pb²⁺ ranking. **DTPA** is clinical for other metals/radionuclides, not standard for lead. |

A chemistry caveat: **intrinsic stability constants are not the same as in vivo effectiveness**. For lead chelation, protonation, calcium competition, route of administration, toxicity, and redistribution risk matter a lot—this is why **CaNa₂EDTA** remains clinically important even though other ligands may also show very high Pb²⁺ affinity in equilibrium datasets.

**Source:** NIST SRD-46.

claims.text: ## Bottom line
| Which ligands in SRD-46 have the highest affinity for Pb²⁺? | **DTPA, CDTA, and EDTA** are the top Pb²⁺ chelator families identified in the retrieved SRD-46 results. |
| Are any used clinically? | **Yes** — **EDTA**, specifically **CaNa₂EDTA**, is the key clinically established chelator for lead poisoning. |

| Any others with clinical relevance? | **D-penicillamine** has clinical history, but it was **not** a top-affinity leader in the retrieved Pb²⁺ ranking. **DTPA** is clinical for other metals/radionuclides, not standard for lead. |
A chemistry caveat:
**intrinsic stability constants are not the same as in vivo effectiveness**
For lead chelation, protonation, calcium competition, route of administration, toxicity, and redistribution risk matter a lot
**CaNa₂EDTA** remains clinically important
even though other ligands may also show very high Pb²⁺ affinity in equilibrium datasets
